seo.title: Ollama vs vLLM vs llama.cpp for Self-Hosted AI
seo.description: Compare local and self-hosted LLM runtimes by setup, hardware, throughput, quantization, API compatibility, scaling, and production fit.

content.intro: Ollama, vLLM, and llama.cpp all serve open models behind application-friendly interfaces, but they optimize for different jobs. Ollama makes local model use approachable, vLLM targets high-throughput GPU serving, and llama.cpp emphasizes portable inference across consumer and edge hardware. Picking the wrong layer often produces either needless operations or a runtime that cannot meet concurrency targets.

options.name: Ollama
options.tagline: Developer-friendly local model runtime and API
options.best_for: Local development, prototypes, desktop workflows, and small services that value simple model management.

options.name: vLLM
options.best_for: GPU-backed production endpoints where continuous batching, throughput, and multi-user serving are primary.

options.name: llama.cpp
options.tagline: Portable C/C++ inference runtime for GGUF models
options.best_for: CPU, Apple Silicon, mixed CPU/GPU, edge, and highly controlled quantized deployments.

criteria.name: Setup experience
criteria.description: Time from installation to a local chat or API call.

values.Ollama: Install the app or binary, pull a model, and run it through CLI or API
values.vLLM: Install a compatible Python/GPU stack and launch a model server
values.llama.cpp: Use packaged binaries or compile, then obtain a compatible GGUF model

criteria.name: Primary hardware
criteria.description: The environment each runtime most naturally targets.

values.Ollama: Developer laptops and workstations across supported operating systems
values.llama.cpp: Wide CPU and GPU range including Apple Silicon and constrained devices

criteria.name: High-concurrency throughput
criteria.description: Efficiency when many users generate simultaneously.

values.Ollama: Suitable for modest serving, but not centered on fleet-scale throughput
values.vLLM: Continuous batching and serving optimizations target high throughput
values.llama.cpp: Server supports parallel decoding and batching, with performance tied closely to hardware and build

criteria.name: Quantized local models
criteria.description: Ease and flexibility of running reduced-precision models on limited hardware.

values.Ollama: Model library and Modelfiles simplify packaged local models
values.vLLM: Supports multiple quantization approaches in GPU-serving workflows
values.llama.cpp: GGUF ecosystem and many quantization levels are core strengths

criteria.name: API compatibility
criteria.description: Ease of connecting existing webapp SDKs.

values.vLLM: OpenAI-compatible server for common generation APIs
values.llama.cpp: llama-server exposes OpenAI-compatible and additional native endpoints

criteria.name: Model operations
criteria.description: How models are obtained, configured, and switched.

values.Ollama: Pullable model library and declarative Modelfiles
values.vLLM: Model identifiers and server flags fit controlled deployment manifests
values.llama.cpp: Direct GGUF file control, conversion tools, and detailed runtime flags

criteria.name: Horizontal scaling
criteria.description: Fit for replicated or distributed production serving.

values.Ollama: Usually wrapped with external orchestration for multi-instance services
values.vLLM: Designed to participate in distributed and production serving stacks
values.llama.cpp: Replicate llama-server externally; cluster orchestration is outside the project

criteria.name: Portability
criteria.description: Breadth of deployment targets and low-level control.

values.Ollama: Good desktop portability with an opinionated runtime
values.vLLM: Focused on supported accelerator and server environments
values.llama.cpp: Broad architectures, acceleration backends, and build-time control

criteria.name: Production security
criteria.description: The minimum hardening required for a web-facing deployment.

values.Ollama: Place behind authenticated application infrastructure; do not expose a developer runtime directly
values.vLLM: Add authentication, quotas, network isolation, request limits, and capacity controls
values.llama.cpp: Bind privately, add an authenticated reverse proxy, sandbox untrusted models, and cap resources

verdict.summary: Start with Ollama for the fastest local feedback loop, graduate to vLLM when GPU utilization and concurrent throughput dominate, and choose llama.cpp when portable quantized inference or exact runtime control matters. Benchmark your own model, context length, prompt distribution, output length, concurrency, and hardware; tokens-per-second figures from a different workload are not capacity plans.

use_cases.scenario: Local development for an AI-coded webapp
use_cases.reason: Model installation, switching, and API access require little infrastructure work.

use_cases.scenario: Production GPU endpoint with many concurrent users
use_cases.reason: Its scheduling and batching model is built around efficient high-throughput serving.

use_cases.scenario: Private inference on a Mac mini or CPU-heavy edge host
use_cases.reason: Portable backends and GGUF quantization make constrained and mixed hardware practical.
